coredata.dc:title: Enhanced antibacterial testing and latent fingerprint detection using dichlorofluorescein-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of King Saud University.

coredata.dc:creator: Thinthasit, Aphinya
Muryadi, Elvaro Islami
Jaya, Syamsun
Nugroho, David
Chanthai, Saksit
Benchawattananon, Rachadaporn
coredata.dc:description: This research presents an innovative multifunctional reagent consisting of dichlorofluorescein-doped carbon dots (CDs-DC) generated from fresh coconut water by a hydrothermal method. This work’s innovation resides in the simultaneous application of CDs-DC for latent fingerprint detection on nonporous surfaces and improved antibacterial efficacy. The incorporation of dichlorofluorescein into the CDs matrix enhances photoluminescent characteristics and improves antibacterial effectiveness against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria (Escherichia coli, Serratia marcescens, Bacillus megaterium, Staphylococcus aureus). The addition of a starch matrix significantly strengthened this combination, enhancing the adherence and recognition of complex fingerprint patterns under UV light. Furthermore, the CDs-DC demonstrated significant reactive oxygen species (ROS) production, enhancing their antibacterial efficacy. This dual-functional device represents a notable progression in forensic science and antibacterial technology, establishing itself as a sustainable and highly efficient instrument for these purposes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Latent fingerprint
Antibacterial activity
Dichlorofluorescein
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) and carbon dots doped with dichlorofluorescein (CDs-DC)
Determination of antioxidant activity using ABTS and DPPH techniques
Method detection of a latent fingerprint
Characteristics of CDs by XPS, FTIR and XRD
Morphology and elemental analysis of CDs and CDs-DC
Optical properties of both CDs and CDs-DC products
Applications of the CDs-DC for antibacterial and antioxidant
Applications of the CDs-DC/starch powder for latent fingerprint detection
Declarations Ethics approval and consent to participate

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank as following: 1. Research Center for Environmental and Hazardous Substance Management (EHSM), Khon Kaen University; 2. Materials Chemistry Research Center (MCRC), Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellence for Innovation Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Khon Kaen University; and 3. Police Forensic Science Central 4, Khon Kaen Province, Khon Kaen City (Pol.Lt.Col. Nissara Panggao, Pol.Lt.Col. Narawut Jaiwijjt and Pol.Lt.Col. Mattika Sukprasert) for the latent fingerprint detection using AFIS Program.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS analysis of CDs: (a) survey spectrum, (b) C1s binding energy, and (c) O1s binding energy. CDs-DC: (d) survey spectrum, (e) C1s binding energy, (f) O1s binding energy and (g) Cl 2p binding energy.
Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of (a) bare CDs (b) CDs-DC (c) bare DC and (d) the XRD of CDs aCDs-DC.
(a) Transmission electron microscope (TEM) of CDs-DC, SEM of (b) CDs-DC, (c) CDs-DC/starch, (d) bare tapioca powder and EDX of (e) CDs and (f) CDs-DC.
(a) UV–Visible absorption spectra of CDs and (b) fluorescence spectra of CDs with various durations.
(a) UV–Visible absorption spectra of various temperatures of CDs and (b) fluorescence spectra of CDs with various temperatures.
(a) Comparison of fluorescence spectra of CDs, CDs-DC, and various concentrations of dicholorofluorescein (b) effect of various pH in CDs-DC.

Comparison of (a) fluorescence spectra for CDs powder, dichlorofluorescein 10 ppm powder and CDs-DC powder (λex 360 nm), (b) CDs powder, dichlorofluorescein 10 ppm powder and CDs-DC powder for latent fingerprint detection. (UV lamp at 365 nm).
Effect of storage time of the CDs-DC powder on the ability to detect the latent fingerprint.

Comparison of the latent fingerprint detection on non-porous surface material (a) CDs-DC under UV lamp (b) Commercial Powder under UV Lamp and (c) Commercial Powder by using AFIS program (AFIS results from collaboration with Police Forensic Science 4, Khon Kaen, Thailand).
Fluorescence as seen in color observed from different carbon dots prepared from various precursors.
Antimicrobial test by agar disk diffusion assay of CDs-DC.
Application of the obtained CDs-DC/starch powder for latent fingerprint detection on some non-porous material surfaces.
Effect of storage time and temperature on the latent fingerprint application on glass, iron, and plastic surfaces.

head.ce:title: Enhanced antibacterial testing and latent fingerprint detection using dichlorofluorescein-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Integrated Science, Faculty of Science, Khon Kaen University, Khon Kaen 40002, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacology, Faculty of Medicine, Khon Kaen University, Khon Kaen 40002, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacology, Faculty of Medicine, Khon Kaen University, Khon Kaen 40002, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Chemistry Research Center, Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellence for Innovation Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Khon Kaen University, Khon Kaen 40002, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellence for Innovation Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Materials Chemistry Research Center, Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellence for Innovation Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Khon Kaen University, Khon Kaen 40002, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research presents an innovative multifunctional reagent consisting of dichlorofluorescein-doped carbon dots (CDs-DC) generated from fresh coconut water by a hydrothermal method. This work’s innovation resides in the simultaneous application of CDs-DC for latent fingerprint detection on nonporous surfaces and improved antibacterial efficacy. The incorporation of dichlorofluorescein into the CDs matrix enhances photoluminescent characteristics and improves antibacterial effectiveness against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria (Escherichia coli, Serratia marcescens, Bacillus megaterium, Staphylococcus aureus). The addition of a starch matrix significantly strengthened this combination, enhancing the adherence and recognition of complex fingerprint patterns under UV light. Furthermore, the CDs-DC demonstrated significant reactive oxygen species (ROS) production, enhancing their antibacterial efficacy. This dual-functional device represents a notable progression in forensic science and antibacterial technology, establishing itself as a sustainable and highly efficient instrument for these purposes.

ce:section.ce:para: Fingerprint analysis is the definitive method for identifying persons in criminal investigations [1]. Biometrics, such as fingerprints, are extensively utilized in law enforcement and legal contexts because of their distinctiveness, simplicity of verification, and durability, barring instances of significant skin injury [2,3]. Fingerprints, consisting of ridges and holes linked to sweat glands, are among the most dependable forms of evidence. There exist three categories of fingerprints: plastic, patent, and latent. Plastic and patent fingerprints are readily observable, whereas latent fingerprints (LFPs) are typically concealed and necessitate chemical reagents for their identification. These reagents interact with residues from fingerprints, rendering invisible prints detectable for forensic examination [4–6]. Investigating the detection of latent fingerprints is crucial for the progression of forensic science and the enhancement of criminal justice. Latent fingerprints are vital evidence in investigations, and improving detection methods can greatly assist in identifying criminals and securing convictions, particularly on nonporous surfaces where conventional approaches may be inadequate. Currently many researchers develop many chemical reagents to detect the LFPs, such as RhB10-cellulose [54], N-CD/hydrotalcite [55], Zn/N-CDs [56], etc. The excessive utilization of antibiotics and the absence of alternative therapies have resulted in a swift rise of infectious disorders attributable to bacteria, fungus, viruses, and parasites. Bacteria independently account for millions of fatalities annually. Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) constitutes a significant public health hazard, primarily attributable to the over utilization of antibiotics, leading to the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria. The protracted advancement of novel pharmaceuticals has exacerbated this dilemma [7–14]. Developing dependable and cost-effective techniques for bacterial detection in patients may mitigate antibiotic abuse. Assays such as ABTS and DPPH are frequently employed to evaluate antioxidant capacities, including those of carbon dots. These assays are crucial for evaluating the efficacy of carbon dots in generating reactive oxygen species (ROS), which compromise microbial cell walls, membranes, and internal structures, resulting in cell death. Furthermore, CDs can engage with bacterial membranes via electrostatic attraction, compromising cellular integrity. This technique may aid fingerprint recognition by suppressing microbial proliferation on the fingerprint surface, hence maintaining the quality and detail of latent prints for extended durations [52,53].

Carbon dots (CDs) have a distinct feature in which their diameters are smaller than 10 nm, and they possess a bonding structure that consists of a combination of sp3 and sp2 hybridization. In addition to their particle size, carbon dots (CDs) possess a fluorescence feature that finds widespread application in diverse fields, including biosensors, bioimaging, and drug administration, among others [45–47,57]. The photoluminescent properties of CDs make them a viable alternative material for use in nanoclusters and traditional dye molecules within the optoelectronics domain. The photoluminescence (PL) feature of carbon dots (CDs) is contingent upon various factors, including the carbon source, synthesis technique, and parameters employed throughout the production process. The utilization of CDs in the fields of optronics, catalytic processes, and sensors is highly promising owing to their exceptional electronic characteristics resulting from the presence of carbon-based quantum dots as electron donors and acceptors. This interaction between the quantum dots induces chemiluminescence and luminescence, as evidenced by previous studies [15–20,32]. Heteroatoms, such as nitrogen and sulfur, have the ability to contribute additional electrons, hence enhancing the intensity of emission. Prior studies have identified several sources for the synthesis of nitrogen (N) atoms, including freshly prepared pear phenylenediamine (o-PD) [12], sugar and urea [26], citric Acid and diethylenetriamine [10], and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) [24]. The advancement of CDs for fingerprint identification has progressed notably owing to their robust fluorescence, adjustable surface characteristics, and synthesis adaptability. Recent developments have concentrated on enhancing sensitivity, selectivity, and adaptability to diverse conditions, including pH and substrate types, through the use of doping methods, surface functionalization, and the synthesis of composites with other nanoparticles. CDs have significant benefits, such as intense fluorescence for high-sensitivity imaging, tunable emission colours, and a robust binding affinity to fingerprint residues, facilitating effective visualisation under difficult settings [58–60]. Nonetheless, obstacles including durability under environmental conditions and potential concerns with cost and large-scale production require additional examination to enhance their practical application in forensic science [61–63].

The green synthesis method can be utilized to synthesize CDs by utilizing natural materials, such watermelon peel, orange, red beetroot, and others, as carbon sources during the process. The categorization of CD synthesis can generally be divided into two main approaches: the “top-down” and “bottom-up” methodologies. The top-down synthesis approach involves the fragmentation of large carbon compounds into smaller segments by techniques such as laser ablation and chemical oxidation. The carbon precursors utilized in bottom-up synthesis are subjected to various heat treatments, including the hydrothermal method, pyrolysis, and microwave assisted method, as documented in Table 1 of the preceding research [21–27].

This study details the straightforward synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from coconut water, which are subsequently combined with dichlorofluorescein to form CDs-DC via hydrothermal methods. The results indicate that CDs-DC can detect LFPs on various non-porous surfaces, across different storage durations and temperatures. Additionally, CDs-DC demonstrate promising antibacterial efficacy against both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria .

The procurement of tapioca flour for commercial purposes was conducted at a traditional market located in Khon Kaen City. The acquisition of coconut water took place at the convenience store located within the Complex and Food Service building of Khon Kaen University. The purchase of anhydrous ethyl alcohol with a purity of 99.9 % was made from Daejung Chemicals & Metals Co., Ltd., located in Siheung, Republic of Korea. The compound Dichloro-(R)-fluorescein was procured from Max & Baker Ltd., a company based in Dagenham, England.

The synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) was conducted using a heating oven (BINDER ED 115UL-120V). The experimental setup involved the utilization of a centrifuge model NF 800 and a magnetic stirrer (specifically, the Daihan Scientific MSH-20D) to facilitate the mixing process between fluorescein-coloured CDs and commercially available tapioca powder. The optical properties of the CDs product were assessed using a commercial UV lamp at a wavelength of 365 nm, as well as using fluorescence and UV–Visible spectrophotometry. The structural characteristics were verified using various analytical techniques, including X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (KRATOS AXIS SUPRA), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) using a Bruker TENSOR 27 instrument, Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX) with an Oxford Instruments X-Maxn detector, Focused Ion Beam Scanning Electron Microscopy (FIB-SEM) performed on a Helios NanoLab G3 CX system, and High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) utilizing a Schottky field emission type electron gun, detection latent fingerprint on-nonporous surface by using Brush (Zephyr brush, BVDA International BV, Netherlands), for comparison result of detection latent fingerprint on non-porous surface materials by using AFIS program under collaboration with Police forensic science central 4 Khon Kaen department, Fingerprint examination and AFIS ROOM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) and carbon dots doped with dichlorofluorescein (CDs-DC)
ce:section.ce:para: A 10 mL volume of coconut water was filtered using a nylon filter membrane with a pore size of 0.22 µm. The resultant filter was subsequently mixed with 10 mL of anhydrous ethyl alcohol in a 1:1 proportion. The synthesis procedure was carried out utilizing the hydrothermal method, wherein the samples were exposed to varied temperatures (200, 180, 160, and 140 °C) and reaction durations (1, 2, 3, and 4 h) in an oven. After the synthesis process was finished, the resultant product was subjected to centrifugation at a speed of 10,000 revolutions per minute for a duration of 10 min. The CDs-DC preparation was carried out as follows: A 10 mL sample of coconut water was filtered using a nylon filter membrane with a pore size of 0.22 µm. Afterwards, 10 mL of dehydrated ethyl alcohol (in a 1:1 proportion) was mixed with the filtered coconut water. Ultimately, a volume of 20 mL of a solution containing dichlorofluorescein at a concentration of 10 parts per million (ppm) was introduced into the combination. The hydrothermal approach was utilized to carry out the synthesis process, using the identical procedure employed for the synthesis of the CDs.

Five grams of tapioca flour powder was combined with 20 mL of the CDs-DC solution, The solution was to magnetic stirring at a temperature of ambient conditions, with a rotational speed of 400 revolutions per minute for 24 h. Subsequently, the sample was dried in oven maintained at a temperature of 75 °C for 24 h. The dried material was grinded and sieving using a 100-mesh sieve. The resulting powder was subsequently stored at room temperature in the desiccator for future utilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of antioxidant activity using ABTS and DPPH techniques
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of CDs and CDs-DC were prepared in a 99 % ethanol solvent, ranging from 0 to 50 µg/mL. These solutions were mixed with 80 µL of ABTS and 580 µL of phosphate buffer in a 96-well plate. The mixture was left in the dark for 20 min, after which the absorbance at 734 nm was measured. The inhibiting effect was calculated using the formula: % Inhibiting effect = (Absorbance Control − Absorbance Sample/Absorbance Control) × 100. Furthermore, a solution of DPPH at a concentration of 60 µg/mL was combined with 99 % ethanol. Subsequently, the CDs and CDs-DC were prepared at different concentrations ranging from 0 to 50 µg/mL in 99 % ethanol solvent. Various amounts of CDs, CDs-DC, and DPPH solution were added to a tube and the mixture was kept in darkness for a duration of 20 min. Subsequently, the absorbance was quantified using a UV–Vis Spectrophotometer. The percentage of scavenging can be calculated using the formula % inhibiting effect = (Absorbance Control – Absorbance Sample/Absorbance Control) × 100, where the wavelength is 517 nm.z.

The bacteria Escherichia coli, Serratia marcescens, Bacillus megaterium, and Staphylococcus aureus were obtained from an inclined medium and cultivated using the 0.5 McFarland method at a temperature of 37 °C for a duration of 24 h. Ultimately, a bacterial suspension with a concentration of 1.5 × 108 (CFU/mL) was acquired for utilization. The N-CDs sample underwent testing utilizing an agar plate assay. A solution containing 3 grammes of medium agar was prepared by diluting it with 100 mL of DI water and sterilizing it using an autoclave. Once the agar medium was prepared, a 3D dimensional swab of each bacteria strain was taken and applied to the agar medium from the stock solution (1.5 × 108 CFU/mL). The results of the CDs-DC with a concentration of 25 % (%wt/v) were compared to the control plates using DI water, 96 % alcohol, and a commercial amoxicillin drug with a concentration of 25 % (%wt/v).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method detection of a latent fingerprint
ce:section.ce:para: Latent fingerprints are deposited on non-porous surfaces by gently touching the forehead for a length of 5 s. The fingerprint was processed utilizing the brush method with the use of the CDs-DC powder product. Ultimately, the previously undetectable fingerprint was effectively recognized and displayed as a clear and precise image of small intricacies when exposed to ultraviolet light. The latent fingerprint detection results were compared using the AFIS program in partnership with the Police Forensic Science Central 4 Khon Kaen department, specifically the Fingerprint Examination and AFIS Room. The Khon Kaen University Ethical Committee N-Coated that ethical approval was not required for this study, as it did not involve external participants. The fingerprints used in this research were obtained from the internal authors, and all methods employed in the study adhered to the appropriate guidelines and regulations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of CDs by XPS, FTIR and XRD
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate the CDs in order to validate their surface functionality. The survey spectrum depicted in Fig. 1 a provides evidence of the presence of carbon (C 1s, 284.08 eV) and oxygen (O1s, 532.08 eV). The atomic percentages of C1s and O1s are determined to be 77.36 % and 22.64 %, respectively. The deconvolution of the C1s spectrum reveals that the binding energy of the sp2 C–C bond is 284.6 eV, the C-O bond has a binding energy of 286.1 eV, and the C=O bond has a binding energy of 288.6 eV (see Fig. 1b). Fig. 1c displays the peak of O1s, which has been subjected to deconvolution analysis. The peaks seen at 532.18 eV are associated with O=C, while the peak at 533.8 eV corresponds to O-C. Additionally, the peak at 534.9 eV suggests the presence of the functional group C-OOH [28,29]. the elemental of CDs-DC also analyst by using XPS and it’s showed in Fig. 1d until Fig. 1g, Fig. 1d show the presence of carbon (C 1s, 282.8 eV), oxygen (O1s, 529.8 eV) and Chlorine (Cl 2p, 199.3 eV), respectively. The deconvolution of the C1s spectrum reveals that the binding energy of the sp2 C–C bond is 285.02 eV, and carbide in the energy 281.9 eV, respectively (Fig. 1e) [39]. Fig. 1f displays the peak of O1s, which has been subjected to deconvolution analysis. The peaks seen at 531.8 eV are associated with C–O–C, and 528.9 eV corresponds to lattice oxygen [40]. Fig. 1g show the deconvolution of chlorine, and it’s shown 2 peaks at 197.6 and 199.3 and it’s assigned to the energy of Cl 2p1/2 and Cl 2p3/2 respectively [41].

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) was employed to identify the functional groups present in the CDs, as depicted in Fig. 2 . In Fig. 2a, it can be shown that CDs-DC exhibits characteristic spectral features. The O–H stretching band is observed at about 3300 cm−1, indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups. The C–H bond stretching vibration is detected at 2920 cm−1, indicating the presence of alkane bonds. Additionally, a prominent peak is observed at 1700 cm−1, indicating the presence of a C = O bond. Furthermore, a peak is observed at 1194 cm−1, indicating the presence of a C–O stretching bond. These spectral features were observed in the carbon dots derived from coconut water [29]. The presence of C–O was also detected at a wavenumber of 1022 cm−1 [30], while the wavenumber of 774 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching bonds of CH2. In Fig. 2b, the FTIR analysis of the CDs sample reveals several characteristic peaks. The O–H stretching vibration is observed at 3300 cm−1, while the C–H stretching of alkene bonds occurs at 2900 cm−1. Additionally, the C–H bonding of aromatic compounds is detected at 1650 cm−1, the C–CHO aldehyde bending is observed at 1319 cm−1, and the C–H bond is present at 1000 cm−1 [31]. Fig. 2c, is show the result of FTIR analysis the DC powder sample. The O–H stretching vibration is observed at 3315 cm−1, while the C–H stretching of alkene bonds occurs at 2929 cm−1. Additionally, the C–H bonding of aromatic compounds is detected at 1629 cm−1, C–O–C is represented on 1010 cm−1 [37] and the C–Cl stretch is present at 766 cm−1 [38]. The characteristic of CDs and CDs-DC also has been study in this research by using X-ray diffraction (XRD), The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis reveals a large hump centered around ∼2θ = 21, which is indicative of the prevailing amorphous nature of the for CDs and CDs-DC (Fig. 2d) [33,34]. This peak is associated with the presence of the carbon phase. The XRD peaks for CDs and CDs-DC exhibit similarity due to the predominance of the carbon dots' amorphous or graphitic structure in the diffraction pattern, with the minor organic dopant not substantially affecting the lattice parameters [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and elemental analysis of CDs and CDs-DC
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 a presents an image examination of the carbon dots (CDs) utilizing a transmission electron microscope (TEM), which serves as a means to validate the findings acquired from the focused ion beam scanning electron microscope (FIB-SEM) picture. The typical diameter of CDs-DC was approximately 2.2 nm. The result of FIB-SEM, as depicted in Fig. 3b, reveals that the coconut water CDs doped with dichlorofluorescein exhibit a notable propensity to melt when exposed to ambient room temperature. Fig. 3c demonstrates that the combination of CDs-DC with fine tapioca flour results in a noticeable increase in the coarseness of the texture of the CDs-DC/starch mixture. The basic property of the bare starch was also compromised (Fig. 3d). The elemental compositions of the CDs and CDs-DC are depicted in Fig. 3e and Fig. 3f, the total carbon %atomic carbon (C) 88.6 % and oxygen (O) comprising 11.35 % of the composition of CDs, and %atomic carbon (C) 75.5 %, oxygen (O) comprising 20.79 % and Chlorine 3.71 % of the composition of CDs-DC, respectively. The elemental compositions of the CDs and CDs-DC also has been analyst by CHN analyst as shown in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of both CDs and CDs-DC products
ce:section.ce:para: Analytical approaches such as UV–Visible absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, and measurement of blue emission under UV radiation were used to explore the optical properties of carbon dots (CDs). This study presents empirical evidence demonstrating that fresh coconut water does not exhibit luminous characteristics when exposed to ultraviolet (UV) radiation. However, when the CDs undergo the hydrothermal process, they exhibit luminous characteristics that vary depending on changes in reaction temperature and duration. The experimental methodology involved observing the state of CDs with different durations when exposed to a UV lamp. The visual attributes of the CDs were documented and are visually depicted in Fig. 4 . When subjected to ultraviolet (UV) radiation, the electron in the lowest energy state of the CDs becomes stimulated as a result of absorbing photons with a maximum wavelength of 380 nm. Subsequently, the electron in an elevated state experiences an energy transfer mechanism, resulting in the emission of a fluorescence spectrum. The CDs produced after one hour did not exhibit any fluorescence under ultraviolet (UV) light. After a period of 2–3 h, the chemical emitted a radiant yellow light. After a duration of 4 h, it had a heightened intensity of blue light and appeared yellowish-brown when seen under visible light. This unique attribute helped to distinguish it from the other samples. The UV–Visible spectrum and photoluminescence spectra of the carbon dots (CDs) were acquired by experimental measurements. The spectrum data in Fig. 4a illustrates the impact of different lengths of synthetic CDs. The results indicate that exposing the sample to a hydrothermal incubation for 4 h resulted in a considerable increase in absorbance in the UV–Visible region. Additionally, there was a noticeable enhancement in fluorescence intensity, as shown in Fig. 4b.

The comparison of fluorescence spectra at various temperatures is illustrated in Fig. 5 . The CDs that were generated displayed a distinct emission peak at a wavelength of 465 nm. The peak exhibited the greatest strength when the temperature reached 200 °C, surpassing the intensity observed in the other samples. The synthesis pathway resulted in the most favorable hydrothermal methodology, characterized by a time span of 4 h and a temperature of 200 °C. Dichlorofluorescein at a concentration of 10 parts per million (ppm) was incorporated into coconut water using the established optimum conditions, employing a same methodology. The experimental findings indicated that the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots (CDs) was amplified upon doping with dichlorofluorescein (CDs-DC), and the peak of dichlorofluorescein (DC) has been disappears the DC structure is already completely reaction with carbon dots as depicted in Fig. 6 (a). The quantum yield (QY) of CDs and CDs-DC has been studied by following methods from a|e – UV–Vis-IR Spectral Software, and it’s showed the CDs and CDs-DC has a quantum yield 29 % and 23 %, Respectively [36]. The impact of solution pH was thoroughly examined. The 30 µL of CDs-DC was added to a 1 mL buffer solution with a pH range of 2–12, and then the volume was increased to 10 mL using DI water. The outcome is displayed in Fig. 6(b). When exposed to acid, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-DC increases. The optimal condition for this action was determined to be at pH 5, resulting in a 150 % increase compared to the control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of the CDs-DC for antibacterial and antioxidant
ce:section.ce:para: The antimicrobial test was conducted on the CDs and CDs-DC using agar diffusion assay. Various gram-negative bacteria (Escherichia coli, Serratia marcescens) and gram-positive bacteria (Bacillus megaterium, Staphylococcus aureus) were used. Ofloxacin, one of the antibiotics in fluoroquinolones family, was used as a positive control due to its broad spectrum – effectively killed both gram-negative bacteria and gram-positive bacteria. The results shown in the Table 3 . The antimicrobial activity of CDs from Coconut water (#2) and CDs-DC (#3) exhibited a similar clear zone result against all bacterial strains. However, CDs-DC demonstrated an optimum condition response compared to CDs and Ethanol 96 %, attributed to its heightened generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). Dichlorofluorescein is recognized for its ability to enhance the generation of ROS through a combination of light-induced photosensitization which is essential for exerting antibacterial effects through possible mechanisms by causing damage to bacterial cell walls, disrupting cell membrane synthesis, inhibiting DNA gyrase synthesis and protein synthesis as shown in Fig. 7 . Furthermore, dichlorofluorescein enhances the stability and solubility of carbon dots, facilitating improved interaction with bacterial cells. Therefore, the utilization of dichlorofluorescein and carbon dots exploits the synergistic effects, resulting in an augmented antibacterial activity. The CDs-DC will retain its capacity to interact with the bacterial cell membrane in the typical manner, causing physical and mechanical harm through two independent methods. The electrostatic interactions enhance surface adhesion, enabling the carbon dots to attach to the bacterial cell membrane. Subsequently, the carbon dots interfere with the bacterial cell wall, so exacerbating the induced harm. The action mechanism involves the disruption and penetration of the bacterial cell membrane, the induction of oxidative stress resulting in DNA/RNA damage, which in turn leads to the alteration or inhibition of crucial gene expressions [42–44]. Table 4 presents a comparison of the antioxidant test results for CDs and CDs-DC. The data indicates that CDs-DC exhibit a better antioxidant capacity compared to CDs, with LC50 values of 4.35 mg/L and 5.97 mg/L for DPPH and ABTS techniques, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of the CDs-DC/starch powder for latent fingerprint detection
ce:section.ce:para: This study aims to examine the detection of latent fingerprints by using a CDs-DC/starch matrix as the experimental technique, with dichlorofluorescein as the control. Dichlorofluorescein is an organic dye that is classified as a member of the fluorescein family. This substance is distinguished by the occurrence of chloride replacements at the 2 and 7 positions, and displays fluorescence upon exposure to ultraviolet (UV) radiation. Fig. 8 illustrates the results of the comparison between CDs-DC/starch powder and dichlorofluorescein powder, both combined with tapioca starch. Fig. 8a illustrates the comparison of fluorescence spectra between CDs/starch powder, CDs-DC/powder, and DC/starch powder. The findings demonstrated that the CDs-DC/starch powder displayed a greater intensity. After the donors placed their fingerprints on the glass surface, several powders were then applied to aid in the identification of hidden fingerprints. In addition, the glass surfaces that had been treated were inspected using ultraviolet (UV) light for further examination. The experimental results indicate that the CDs-DC/starch powder exhibits improved visibility when exposed to a UV light, allowing for the detection of more intricate features of the latent fingerprint compared to the use of 10 ppm of DC dye. Furthermore, the CDs-DC/starch powder demonstrates a greater intensity of fluorescence, as depicted in Fig. 8b. Upon combining the CDs-DC compound with tapioca flour and analyzing it using UV light, it was seen that the starch particles successfully stuck to the fatty residues found in the fingerprint. This contact happened via hydrogen bonding processes, where the starch served as a substrate for detecting latent fingerprints. The hydrophilic constituents of the fingerprint, including sweat, water, and amino acids, establish hydrogen bonds with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the CDs-DC surface, whereas the hydrophobic lipid and fatty acid constituents engage through van der Waals and hydrophobic interactions. The starch matrix utilized in the composition facilitates adhesion to greasy residues, hence enhancing overall attachment to fingerprint ridges [48–51]. Upon binding, the CDs-DC emit fluorescence under UV light owing to the presence of dichlorofluorescein, enabling distinct imaging of fingerprint patterns. The powder formulation was carried out using the methods described in a previous work [35], which intended to detect hidden fingerprints on smooth surfaces that do not absorb liquids. After applying CDs-DC/starch powder to the surface samples to identify latent fingerprints, the materials were inspected using a UV lamp. The results of the powder application, as shown in Table 5 , indicate its effectiveness in identifying more visible latent fingerprints on different surface materials when subjected to ultraviolet (UV) light. This study aimed to analyze the resilience and durability of the carbon dots powder. The study conducted a comparison between the CDs-DC/starch powder manufactured on the first day and the powder that was stored for 28 days at room temperature. The findings illustrated in Fig. 9 demonstrate that the CDs-DC/starch powder continues to be successful in identifying hidden fingerprints, albeit there is a reduction in the brightness of the emitted fluorescence compared to the product's initial freshness. We conducted studies to examine the influence of different temperatures and durations on the storage capability of CDs-DC powder, particularly in terms of its ability to detect latent fingerprints. An unintentional fingerprint was placed on a surface by a volunteer, and then the surface was exposed to regulated temperature conditions of −4 °C and 60 °C in the Forensic Science laboratory at Khon Kaen University. In addition, the sample was kept at a temperature of −4 °C before being analyzed using CDs-DC powder. The material surface underwent a drying and heating procedure in an oven maintained at a temperature of 60 °C for a period of 30 min. The findings on the use of CDs-DC powder for detecting latent fingerprints on different material surfaces are presented in Table 6 . The table provides a concise elucidation of the abbreviations employed: DM represents the accurate identification of small details in the fingerprint, D indicates the identification of the fingerprint without distinct small details, and ND indicates the inability to identify the fingerprint. This study aimed to compare the effectiveness of CDs-DC /starch powder and commercial black powder for detecting latent fingerprints. Additionally, the performance of CDs-Dc was also evaluated and compared using an AFIS program for fingerprint detection. After the volunteer rubbed the head for 3 s, the latent fingerprint was discovered using the brushing method on a non-porous surface. This study entailed the juxtaposition of a concealed fingerprint with black powder that is readily obtainable in the market. Fig. 10 demonstrates that the CDs-DC powder displayed a greater degree of brightness under ultraviolet (UV) light, as compared to the powder that is commercially available. Furthermore, the CDs-DC powder exhibited more intricate details. The comparison between CDs-DC under Uv Lamp with AFIS software reveals that CDs exhibit a similar level of microscopic detail as latent fingerprints on non-porous surfaces (glass surfaces).

This research effectively synthesized dichlorofluorescein-doped carbon dots (CDs-DC) from fresh coconut water using a hydrothermal procedure, demonstrating an efficient and sustainable approach for generating high-quality luminous materials. The characterization of the CDs-DC indicated a particle size of around 2.2 nm, exhibiting superior photoluminescent characteristics relative to undoped carbon dots. The antibacterial experiments indicated that the CDs-DC shown considerable antimicrobial efficacy against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, including Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli, with inhibition zones equal to or exceeding those of traditional antibiotics. This augmented activity is ascribed to the heightened production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which compromise bacterial cell walls and obstruct DNA synthesis, hence enhancing their overall antibacterial effectiveness. Moreover, the utilization of CDs-DC in latent fingerprint identification produced exceptional outcomes. The CDs-DC, when integrated with a starch powder, markedly enhanced the visibility of latent fingerprints on diverse nonporous surfaces, such as glass and plastic, under UV illumination. The detection procedure led to the recognition of complex fingerprint characteristics that were previously imperceptible. This study's findings highlight the potential of CDs-DC as a multifunctional material with significant applications in forensic science and antimicrobial, facilitating further investigation into their clinical and investigative uses.

Material preparation and data collection were performed by Aphinya Thinthasit and David Nugroho. Data analysis was performed by Aphinya Thinthasit. Saksit Chanthai advised guide line of the study design and supervision. Conceptualization was performed by David Nugroho, Syamsun Jaya, Saksit Chanthai, Elvaro Islami Muryadi and Rachadaporn Benchawattananon. The first draft of the manuscript was written by Aphinya Thinthasit, and then Saksit Chanthai proved all review and did editing its final draft. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.
All data in this research are available from the corresponding authors by request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declarations Ethics approval and consent to participate
ce:section.ce:para: Not applicable.
The authors approve the publication of this manuscript.

Aphinya Thinthasit: Investigation, Formal analysis. Elvaro Islami Muryadi: Supervision, Methodology. Syamsun Jaya: Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. David Nugroho: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Saksit Chanthai: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Rachadaporn Benchawattananon: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank as following: 1. Research Center for Environmental and Hazardous Substance Management (EHSM), Khon Kaen University; 2. Materials Chemistry Research Center (MCRC), Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellence for Innovation Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Khon Kaen University; and 3. Police Forensic Science Central 4, Khon Kaen Province, Khon Kaen City (Pol.Lt.Col. Nissara Panggao, Pol.Lt.Col. Narawut Jaiwijjt and Pol.Lt.Col. Mattika Sukprasert) for the latent fingerprint detection using AFIS Program.
